nodes.label: Ancient Philosophy
nodes.summary: Western philosophy from Thales (~624 BCE) to the fall of Rome (476 CE). The birth of rational inquiry — cosmology, ethics, logic, and metaphysics emerged here.

nodes.label: Medieval Philosophy
nodes.summary: Philosophy from Augustine (~400 CE) to the Renaissance (~1500). Faith meets reason — Scholasticism, Islamic Golden Age, and Jewish philosophy shaped the synthesis.

nodes.label: Modern Philosophy
nodes.summary: From Descartes (1637) to Hegel (1831). The age of epistemology — Rationalism, Empiricism, and the Kantian synthesis reshaped what we can know and how.

nodes.label: Contemporary Philosophy
nodes.summary: 20th-21st century movements that cross the analytic-continental divide — Pragmatism, Feminism, Critical Theory, and applied ethics for a technological age.

nodes.label: Eastern Philosophy
nodes.summary: Philosophical traditions of China, India, Japan, and the Islamic world — Confucianism, Taoism, Buddhism, Vedanta, and Islamic philosophy spanning 3000 years.

nodes.label: Analytic Philosophy
nodes.summary: The tradition from Frege and Russell onward — logical analysis, linguistic precision, and formal methods applied to philosophical problems. Dominant in Anglophone academia.

nodes.label: Continental Philosophy
nodes.summary: The European tradition from Kierkegaard through Postmodernism — Existentialism, Phenomenology, Hermeneutics, Critical Theory, and Deconstruction.

nodes.label: Pre-Socratic Philosophy
nodes.summary: The first Western philosophers (6th-5th century BCE). They asked: what is everything made of? Thales said water, Heraclitus said fire and flux, Parmenides said change is illusion, Democritus said atoms.

nodes.label: Heraclitus
nodes.summary: Heraclitus of Ephesus (~535-475 BCE). 'Everything flows' (panta rhei). Reality is constant change governed by the Logos. You cannot step into the same river twice. Unity of opposites.

nodes.label: Parmenides
nodes.summary: Parmenides of Elea (~515-450 BCE). Being is one, unchanging, and eternal. Change and motion are illusions. His rigorous deductive method influenced all subsequent metaphysics.

nodes.label: Democritus
nodes.summary: Democritus of Abdera (~460-370 BCE). Co-founder of atomism with Leucippus. Reality is atoms and void. All qualities emerge from the arrangement of indivisible particles — anticipating modern physics by 2400 years.

nodes.label: Socrates
nodes.summary: Socrates of Athens (470-399 BCE). Wrote nothing; known through Plato's dialogues. Invented the Socratic method — relentless questioning to expose ignorance. 'The unexamined life is not worth living.' Executed for impiety and corrupting youth.

nodes.label: Plato
nodes.summary: Plato (428-348 BCE). Student of Socrates, founded the Academy. Theory of Forms: true reality is abstract, eternal, perfect Ideas — the physical world is mere shadow. Allegory of the Cave. Philosopher-kings should rule.

nodes.label: Aristotle
nodes.summary: Aristotle (384-322 BCE). Student of Plato, tutor to Alexander. Systematized logic, ethics, politics, biology, metaphysics. Rejected Plato's separate Forms — universals exist in particulars. Founded the Lyceum.

nodes.label: Stoicism
nodes.summary: Founded by Zeno of Citium (~300 BCE). Virtue is the only good; external events are indifferent. Live according to nature and reason. Control what you can, accept what you cannot. Thrived from Athens to Rome.

nodes.label: Epictetus
nodes.summary: Epictetus (~50-135 CE). Born a slave, became one of the greatest Stoic teachers. 'It is not things that disturb us, but our judgments about things.' His Discourses and Enchiridion are practical handbooks for living.

nodes.label: Marcus Aurelius
nodes.summary: Marcus Aurelius (121-180 CE). Roman Emperor and Stoic philosopher. His Meditations — private journal never meant for publication — remains the most intimate portrait of philosophical practice ever written.

nodes.label: Epicureanism
nodes.summary: Founded by Epicurus (~341-270 BCE). Pleasure (ataraxia — tranquility) is the highest good, but true pleasure is the absence of pain, not hedonistic excess. Atomist physics, no fear of death or gods.

nodes.label: Cynicism
nodes.summary: Founded by Antisthenes and Diogenes of Sinope (~4th century BCE). Reject convention, live according to nature, pursue virtue through radical simplicity. Diogenes lived in a barrel and told Alexander to move out of his sunlight.

nodes.label: Ancient Skepticism
nodes.summary: Pyrrho of Elis (~360-270 BCE) and later Academic Skeptics. Suspend judgment (epoche) on all claims — neither affirm nor deny. True peace of mind comes from abandoning the pursuit of certainty.

nodes.label: Neoplatonism
nodes.summary: Late ancient synthesis of Plato with mystical and metaphysical elements (3rd-6th century CE). Reality emanates from the One through stages of decreasing perfection. Profoundly influenced Christianity, Islam, and Judaism.

nodes.label: Plotinus
nodes.summary: Plotinus (204-270 CE). Founder of Neoplatonism. Reality flows from the One (absolute unity) through Nous (intellect) to Soul to the material world. Mystical union with the One is the ultimate goal.

nodes.label: Augustine of Hippo
nodes.summary: Augustine (354-430 CE). Fused Neoplatonism with Christianity. Original sin, divine grace, the City of God. His Confessions invented autobiography. Dominated Western theology for a millennium.

nodes.label: Thomas Aquinas
nodes.summary: Thomas Aquinas (1225-1274). Synthesized Aristotle with Christianity in the Summa Theologiae. Five Ways to prove God's existence. Natural law ethics. Faith and reason are compatible — the crowning achievement of Scholasticism.

nodes.label: Scholasticism
nodes.summary: The dominant method of medieval universities (11th-15th century). Rigorous dialectical reasoning applied to theological questions. Disputatio, quaestio, and commentary on authorities — especially Aristotle and the Bible.

nodes.label: Avicenna (Ibn Sina)
nodes.summary: Avicenna (980-1037 CE). Persian polymath — philosopher, physician, astronomer. His Canon of Medicine and The Cure shaped both Islamic and Latin philosophy. Essence-existence distinction. The Flying Man thought experiment.

nodes.label: Averroes (Ibn Rushd)
nodes.summary: Averroes (1126-1198 CE). The great Aristotelian commentator from Cordoba. Defended philosophy against al-Ghazali's attacks. His commentaries on Aristotle dominated European universities for centuries.

nodes.label: Maimonides
nodes.summary: Moses Maimonides (1138-1204). Greatest medieval Jewish philosopher. Guide for the Perplexed reconciled Aristotelian philosophy with Jewish theology. Negative theology — God is known by what He is not.

nodes.label: William of Ockham
nodes.summary: William of Ockham (~1287-1347). Nominalist who denied the reality of universals. Ockham's Razor: 'Entities must not be multiplied beyond necessity.' His parsimony principle became foundational to modern science.

nodes.label: Medieval Mysticism
nodes.summary: Meister Eckhart, Hildegard of Bingen, Julian of Norwich, and others (~12th-15th century). Direct experience of the divine beyond rational theology. Influenced by Neoplatonism and Pseudo-Dionysius.

nodes.label: Rationalism
nodes.summary: The 17th-century school holding that reason, not sense experience, is the primary source of knowledge. Innate ideas exist. Descartes, Spinoza, and Leibniz built grand metaphysical systems from pure thought.

nodes.label: Descartes
nodes.summary: Rene Descartes (1596-1650). Father of modern philosophy. Methodical doubt stripped away all beliefs until 'Cogito ergo sum' — I think, therefore I am. Mind-body dualism. Analytic geometry.

nodes.label: Spinoza
nodes.summary: Baruch Spinoza (1632-1677). God and Nature are one substance (Deus sive Natura). Everything follows necessarily from God's nature. Ethics demonstrated in geometric order. Radical monism, determinism, and intellectual love of God.

nodes.label: Leibniz
nodes.summary: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646-1716). Reality consists of monads — indivisible, windowless units of perception. This is the best of all possible worlds (theodicy). Co-invented calculus. Universal logical language.

nodes.label: Empiricism
nodes.summary: The British school holding that all knowledge derives from sense experience. No innate ideas — the mind begins as a blank slate. Locke, Berkeley, and Hume pushed this principle to increasingly radical conclusions.

nodes.label: John Locke
nodes.summary: John Locke (1632-1704). The mind is a tabula rasa — all knowledge comes from experience (sensation and reflection). Natural rights to life, liberty, property. Social contract. Father of classical liberalism.

nodes.label: David Hume
nodes.summary: David Hume (1711-1776). The great skeptic. Causation is mere habit, not logical necessity. The is-ought gap in ethics. Undermined rational theology, induction, and personal identity. Woke Kant from his 'dogmatic slumber.'

nodes.label: George Berkeley
nodes.summary: George Berkeley (1685-1753). Esse est percipi — to be is to be perceived. Material objects exist only as ideas in minds. Immaterialism: God's perpetual perception sustains reality. Radical empiricism taken to idealist conclusions.

nodes.label: Immanuel Kant
nodes.summary: Immanuel Kant (1724-1804). Synthesized Rationalism and Empiricism in the Critique of Pure Reason. Space, time, and causation are forms imposed by the mind. The categorical imperative in ethics. Changed everything.

nodes.label: Utilitarianism
nodes.summary: The moral theory that the right action maximizes overall happiness. Founded by Bentham's felicific calculus and refined by Mill's qualitative distinctions. The most influential consequentialist ethics.

nodes.label: Jeremy Bentham
nodes.summary: Jeremy Bentham (1748-1832). Founder of utilitarianism. 'The greatest happiness of the greatest number.' Felicific calculus to measure pleasure and pain. Legal reformer, animal rights advocate, his skeleton still displayed at UCL.

nodes.label: John Stuart Mill
nodes.summary: John Stuart Mill (1806-1873). Refined utilitarianism with qualitative distinctions — 'better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied.' On Liberty defended individual freedom. Champion of women's rights.

nodes.label: German Idealism
nodes.summary: Post-Kantian movement (1780s-1830s). Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel sought to complete Kant's project by unifying subject and object, mind and world, in grand systematic philosophies of Spirit.

nodes.label: Hegel
nodes.summary: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel (1770-1831). The dialectic: thesis, antithesis, synthesis. History is the self-realization of Spirit (Geist). The rational is real. Most ambitious philosophical system since Aristotle — and the most contested.

nodes.label: Schopenhauer
nodes.summary: Arthur Schopenhauer (1788-1860). The world is Will — blind, purposeless striving. Life is suffering; desire is insatiable. Escape through art, compassion, and ascetic denial. Influenced Nietzsche, Wagner, and Freud.

nodes.label: Existentialism
nodes.summary: The philosophy of radical freedom and individual existence (19th-20th century). Existence precedes essence. Authenticity, anxiety, absurdity, and the burden of choice. From Kierkegaard to Sartre and Camus.

nodes.label: Kierkegaard
nodes.summary: Soren Kierkegaard (1813-1855). Father of existentialism. The leap of faith. Three stages of existence: aesthetic, ethical, religious. Subjectivity is truth. Attacked Hegel's system for ignoring the individual.

nodes.label: Nietzsche
nodes.summary: Friedrich Nietzsche (1844-1900). God is dead — and we killed him. Will to power, eternal recurrence, the Ubermensch. Master and slave morality. Demolished comfortable certainties; created the crisis of meaning that defines modernity.

nodes.label: Heidegger
nodes.summary: Martin Heidegger (1889-1976). Being and Time (1927) asked: what does it mean to be? Dasein (being-there), thrownness, being-toward-death, authenticity. Revolutionized ontology. His Nazi involvement remains deeply troubling.

nodes.label: Sartre
nodes.summary: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980). Existence precedes essence — we are condemned to be free. Being and Nothingness. Bad faith is self-deception about our freedom. Engaged intellectual, declined the Nobel Prize.

nodes.label: Camus
nodes.summary: Albert Camus (1913-1960). The Myth of Sisyphus: life is absurd, but we must imagine Sisyphus happy. The Stranger, The Plague. Rejected existentialist label; broke with Sartre over political violence.

nodes.label: Phenomenology
nodes.summary: The study of structures of experience as they appear to consciousness. Founded by Husserl's 'to the things themselves!' Intentionality — consciousness is always consciousness of something.

nodes.label: Husserl
nodes.summary: Edmund Husserl (1859-1938). Founder of phenomenology. Logical Investigations and Ideas. Phenomenological reduction (epoche) brackets the natural attitude to reveal pure consciousness. Influenced Heidegger, Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, and Derrida.

nodes.label: Marxism
nodes.summary: Philosophy of historical materialism, class struggle, and revolutionary praxis. Marx inverted Hegel — material conditions, not ideas, drive history. Alienation, ideology, and the critique of capitalism.

nodes.label: Karl Marx
nodes.summary: Karl Marx (1818-1883). Historical materialism, dialectical materialism, surplus value, alienation. The Communist Manifesto and Das Kapital. Turned Hegel on his head — the material world shapes consciousness, not the reverse.

nodes.label: Postmodernism
nodes.summary: Late 20th-century critique of grand narratives, objective truth, and Enlightenment reason. Everything is text, power shapes knowledge, meaning is unstable. Foucault, Derrida, Lyotard, Baudrillard.

nodes.label: Foucault
nodes.summary: Michel Foucault (1926-1984). Power/knowledge — power produces truth, not just represses it. Discipline and Punish, History of Sexuality. Genealogy of institutions: prisons, asylums, clinics. Discourse shapes what we can think.

nodes.label: Derrida
nodes.summary: Jacques Derrida (1930-2004). Deconstruction — texts undermine their own claims. There is nothing outside the text (il n'y a pas de hors-texte). Differance, logocentrism, binary oppositions. The most controversial philosopher of the 20th century.

nodes.label: Mathematical Logic
nodes.summary: The formalization of reasoning using mathematical methods. From Frege's predicate calculus through Russell's logicism to Godel's incompleteness. The foundation of analytic philosophy and computer science.

nodes.label: Frege
nodes.summary: Gottlob Frege (1848-1925). Father of analytic philosophy and modern logic. Begriffsschrift (1879) invented predicate logic with quantifiers. Sense (Sinn) vs reference (Bedeutung). Failed logicist program undermined by Russell's Paradox.

nodes.label: Bertrand Russell
nodes.summary: Bertrand Russell (1872-1970). Principia Mathematica (with Whitehead) attempted to derive all mathematics from logic. Theory of descriptions, logical atomism. Nobel Prize in Literature. Pacifist, activist, popularizer of philosophy.

nodes.label: Early Wittgenstein
nodes.summary: Ludwig Wittgenstein, Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (1921). The world is the totality of facts, not things. Language pictures reality. 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.' Believed he had solved all problems of philosophy.

nodes.label: Logical Positivism
nodes.summary: The Vienna Circle (1920s-30s): Schlick, Carnap, Neurath. Verification principle — a statement is meaningful only if empirically verifiable or analytically true. Metaphysics is literally nonsensical. Hugely influential, then dismantled by Quine and others.

nodes.label: Ordinary Language Philosophy
nodes.summary: Oxford philosophy of the 1940s-60s: Austin, Ryle, Strawson. Philosophical problems arise from misunderstanding how language ordinarily works. 'Don't ask for the meaning, ask for the use.' Later Wittgenstein's inheritance.

nodes.label: Late Wittgenstein
nodes.summary: Ludwig Wittgenstein, Philosophical Investigations (1953, posthumous). Repudiated his own Tractatus. Language games, family resemblance, rule-following paradox, private language argument. Meaning is use. The beetle in the box.

nodes.label: Philosophy of Mind
nodes.summary: What is the mind? Behaviorism (Ryle), identity theory (Smart), functionalism (Putnam), eliminativism (Churchlands). The hard problem of consciousness (Chalmers). Chinese Room (Searle). Central to AI debates.

nodes.label: Philosophy of Science
nodes.summary: What makes science scientific? Demarcation, explanation, theory change, realism vs anti-realism. From the Vienna Circle through Popper's falsificationism to Kuhn's paradigm shifts and Lakatos's research programmes.

nodes.label: Karl Popper
nodes.summary: Karl Popper (1902-1994). Falsificationism — science advances not by verifying but by trying to refute. Conjectures and Refutations. The Open Society and Its Enemies attacked Plato, Hegel, and Marx. Critical rationalism.

nodes.label: Thomas Kuhn
nodes.summary: Thomas Kuhn (1922-1996). The Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962). Normal science operates within paradigms; anomalies accumulate until a paradigm shift occurs. Incommensurability between paradigms. Changed how we understand science.

nodes.label: Analytic Ethics
nodes.summary: Moral philosophy in the analytic tradition. Meta-ethics (what is moral language?), normative ethics (what should we do?), applied ethics. From Moore's naturalistic fallacy to Rawls's justice and Singer's effective altruism.

nodes.label: John Rawls
nodes.summary: John Rawls (1921-2002). A Theory of Justice (1971). The veil of ignorance: design society not knowing your place in it. Justice as fairness, difference principle, political liberalism. Revived political philosophy.

nodes.label: Confucianism
nodes.summary: Founded by Confucius (551-479 BCE). Ren (benevolence), li (ritual propriety), xiao (filial piety). Moral cultivation through education and social harmony. The Analects. Dominant philosophy of China for over two millennia.

nodes.label: Taoism
nodes.summary: Laozi (6th century BCE) and Zhuangzi (4th century BCE). The Tao that can be told is not the eternal Tao. Wu wei (non-action), naturalness, spontaneity. The Tao Te Ching. Water is soft yet overcomes the hard.

nodes.label: Buddhist Philosophy
nodes.summary: Siddhartha Gautama (~5th century BCE). Four Noble Truths, Eightfold Path. Dependent origination (pratityasamutpada), no-self (anatman), impermanence (anicca). Split into Theravada, Mahayana, and Vajrayana traditions.

nodes.label: Nagarjuna
nodes.summary: Nagarjuna (~150-250 CE). Founder of Madhyamaka (Middle Way) school. Sunyata (emptiness) — all things lack inherent existence. Mulamadhyamakakarika. The most important Buddhist philosopher after the Buddha himself.

nodes.label: Zen Philosophy
nodes.summary: Chan/Zen Buddhism (~6th century CE onward). Direct pointing at the mind, seeing one's nature, becoming Buddha. Koans, zazen, satori. Bodhidharma brought Chan to China; it became Zen in Japan. Beyond words and concepts.

nodes.label: Hindu Philosophy
nodes.summary: Six orthodox schools (darshanas) of Indian philosophy: Nyaya, Vaisheshika, Samkhya, Yoga, Mimamsa, Vedanta. Rooted in the Vedas and Upanishads. Spanning 3000 years of metaphysical, epistemological, and ethical inquiry.

nodes.label: Vedanta
nodes.summary: The culmination of Vedic philosophy. Shankara's Advaita (non-dualism): Brahman alone is real, the world is maya, the self (Atman) is Brahman. Ramanuja's qualified non-dualism and Madhva's dualism offer alternatives.

nodes.label: Yoga Philosophy
nodes.summary: Patanjali's Yoga Sutras (~2nd century BCE). Eight limbs of yoga. Chitta vritti nirodha — cessation of mental fluctuations. Union of individual consciousness with universal consciousness. Far more than physical postures.

nodes.label: Islamic Philosophy
nodes.summary: Falsafa — the Islamic philosophical tradition rooted in Greek thought (8th-12th century). Al-Kindi, al-Farabi, Avicenna, al-Ghazali, Averroes. Preserved and transformed Aristotle, shaped medieval European thought.

nodes.label: Al-Ghazali
nodes.summary: Abu Hamid al-Ghazali (1058-1111). The Incoherence of the Philosophers attacked Avicenna and the Falsafa. Defended Ash'ari theology. Then withdrew to Sufi mysticism. Deeply shaped the relationship between reason and revelation in Islam.

nodes.label: Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
nodes.summary: Ibn Rushd / Averroes (1126-1198). The Incoherence of the Incoherence defended philosophy against al-Ghazali. Greatest Aristotelian commentator. Double truth theory (disputed). More influential in Europe than in the Islamic world.

nodes.label: Pragmatism
nodes.summary: America's original philosophy (1870s onward). Truth is what works — ideas are tools for navigating experience. Peirce, James, Dewey. Anti-foundationalist, pluralist, meliorist. Later revived by Rorty, Putnam, and Brandom.

nodes.label: William James
nodes.summary: William James (1842-1910). Pragmatism, radical empiricism, The Varieties of Religious Experience. Truth is what 'works' in practice. Stream of consciousness. Father of American psychology and pragmatist philosophy.

nodes.label: John Dewey
nodes.summary: John Dewey (1859-1952). Democracy and Education. Instrumentalism — inquiry is problem-solving, not mirror-of-nature. Experience is interaction with environment. Progressive education, public philosophy, democratic experimentalism.

nodes.label: Feminist Philosophy
nodes.summary: Philosophy through the lens of gender. Beauvoir's 'One is not born, but becomes a woman.' Standpoint theory, ethics of care, intersectionality. Challenges the implicit male bias in the Western canon.

nodes.label: Critical Theory
nodes.summary: The Frankfurt School: Horkheimer, Adorno, Marcuse, Habermas (1930s onward). Critique of instrumental reason, culture industry, and late capitalism. Neo-Marxist diagnosis of modernity's pathologies. Habermas's communicative rationality.

nodes.label: Effective Altruism
nodes.summary: Using evidence and reason to determine the most effective ways to do good (2010s). Peter Singer, Toby Ord, William MacAskill. Utilitarian roots applied to global poverty, animal welfare, existential risk, and AI safety.

nodes.label: Philosophy of AI
nodes.summary: Can machines think? Turing Test, Chinese Room argument, symbol grounding problem, AI alignment. From Turing (1950) to the alignment crisis of the 2020s. The most consequential applied philosophy of our time.

nodes.label: Environmental Ethics
nodes.summary: Does nature have intrinsic value? Deep ecology (Naess), land ethic (Leopold), animal liberation (Singer). Anthropocene philosophy — responsibility for climate change, biodiversity loss, and future generations.

edges.label: Plato was Socrates' student — Socrates' execution drove Plato to write the dialogues and found the Academy
Aristotle studied at Plato's Academy for 20 years, then rejected the Theory of Forms while preserving the systematic ambition
Stoics traced their lineage through Socrates — his calm acceptance of death was their ethical ideal
Antisthenes, founder of Cynicism, was a student of Socrates who radicalized his master's indifference to wealth
Zeno of Citium studied under the Cynic Crates before founding Stoicism — preserved Cynic ethics, added physics and logic
Epicurus adopted Democritus's atomic physics as the foundation for his materialist worldview
Plotinus saw himself as the true interpreter of Plato — transformed the Forms into emanations from the One
Augustine converted through Neoplatonism — fused Plotinus's metaphysics with Christian theology
Aquinas called Aristotle 'The Philosopher' and built the Summa Theologiae on Aristotelian foundations
Aquinas relied heavily on Averroes' Aristotelian commentaries — called him 'The Commentator'
Avicenna's essence-existence distinction and proof of God's existence profoundly shaped Aquinas's metaphysics
Averroes wrote the most comprehensive commentaries on Aristotle in the medieval world
Avicenna's philosophy is fundamentally Aristotelian, enriched with Neoplatonic emanation theory
Avicenna's emanation cosmology derives from Neoplatonic sources filtered through al-Farabi
Maimonides reconciled Aristotelian philosophy with Jewish theology in the Guide for the Perplexed
Maimonides engaged deeply with Avicenna's proofs and metaphysics in formulating his own positions
Medieval mysticism drew heavily on Neoplatonic ideas of emanation and union with the divine One
Descartes was educated by Jesuits in the Scholastic tradition — his methodical doubt was a reaction against it
Spinoza radicalized Descartes — collapsed his two substances (mind and matter) into one substance: God/Nature
Leibniz responded to Descartes's physics and metaphysics, proposing monads as a better solution to the mind-body problem
Hume pushed Locke's empiricism to its radical skeptical conclusions — dissolving causation, self, and induction
Berkeley radicalized Locke — if all we know is ideas, why posit material substance at all? Only minds and ideas exist
Hume's skepticism woke Kant from his 'dogmatic slumber' — the Critique of Pure Reason is a direct response
Kant synthesized Rationalism and Empiricism — the mind structures experience, but content comes from the senses
Hegel sought to complete Kant's project — overcoming the thing-in-itself through dialectical reason and absolute knowledge
Schopenhauer took Kant's thing-in-itself and identified it as blind Will — the world as will and representation
Mill was Bentham's intellectual heir — refined utilitarianism by introducing qualitative distinctions between pleasures
Nietzsche's early work was shaped by Schopenhauer's pessimism — he later overcame it with the will to power and affirmation of life
Heidegger was Husserl's student and successor — Being and Time transformed phenomenology into existential ontology
Sartre adapted Heidegger's ontology for existentialism — Being and Nothingness reworks Dasein as pour-soi
Sartre studied Husserl in Berlin — adopted intentionality as the basis for his theory of consciousness
Camus engaged with Kierkegaard's leap of faith — and rejected it. The absurd must be confronted without hope of transcendence
Marx turned Hegel on his head — kept the dialectic but replaced Spirit with material conditions as the driver of history
Foucault's genealogical method is directly inspired by Nietzsche's Genealogy of Morals — power, not truth, drives knowledge
Derrida's first works deconstructed Husserl — showing that the presence Husserl sought is always already deferred
Derrida radicalized Heidegger's destruction of metaphysics into deconstruction — the end of onto-theology
Russell discovered Frege's work and extended his logicist program — then broke it with Russell's Paradox
Wittgenstein came to Cambridge to study with Russell — the Tractatus grew from Russell's logical atomism
The Vienna Circle took the Tractatus as their bible — though Wittgenstein thought they misunderstood it
The Philosophical Investigations is a sustained self-critique — Wittgenstein demolished his own Tractatus
Popper engaged deeply with the Vienna Circle but rejected verification — falsification replaced it as the demarcation criterion
Kuhn challenged Popper's falsificationism — scientists don't abandon paradigms at the first anomaly
Rawls's veil of ignorance is a Kantian thought experiment — justice as fairness derives from the categorical imperative's universalizability
The Frankfurt School is neo-Marxist — updating Marx's critique of capitalism with psychoanalysis and cultural criticism
Effective altruism is applied utilitarianism — maximizing well-being through evidence-based giving and career choice
Beauvoir applied Sartre's existentialism to gender — 'One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman'
Chan/Zen Buddhism absorbed Taoist concepts of naturalness, spontaneity, and non-conceptual awareness
Ibn Rushd's Incoherence of the Incoherence was a direct response to al-Ghazali's attack on philosophy
The foundational opposition: Heraclitus (everything changes) vs Parmenides (nothing changes). All later metaphysics responds to this tension
The great early modern debate: is knowledge from reason (innate ideas) or experience (blank slate)? Kant synthesized both
The 20th-century divide: logic and language analysis vs phenomenology and historical interpretation. The gap is narrowing
Hume denied necessary causal knowledge; Kant replied that causation is a synthetic a priori category the mind imposes on experience
Kierkegaard attacked Hegel's system for swallowing the individual — existence cannot be captured in abstract dialectics
Camus and Sartre broke over political violence and the nature of revolt — Camus rejected Sartre's Marxist commitments
Al-Ghazali's Incoherence of the Philosophers attacked Avicenna's Aristotelian metaphysics as incompatible with Islamic revelation
Ibn Rushd defended philosophy against al-Ghazali's attacks — the Incoherence of the Incoherence is a point-by-point refutation
Kuhn showed that Popper's falsificationism does not match how science actually works — normal science tolerates anomalies
The Investigations explicitly repudiates the Tractatus — meaning is use, not logical picturing. One philosopher, two incompatible visions
Schopenhauer called Hegel a charlatan — rejected dialectical reason for blind irrational Will as the essence of reality
Marx inverted Hegel — material conditions determine consciousness, not the other way around. Dialectical materialism replaces dialectical idealism
Rival Hellenistic schools: Stoics sought virtue through reason and duty; Epicureans sought tranquility through pleasure and retreat
Nagarjuna's emptiness (sunyata) denies any permanent self or essence — directly opposing Vedanta's eternal Atman/Brahman
Carnap singled out Heidegger's 'The Nothing nothings' as the exemplar of meaningless metaphysical pseudo-statements
Stoics adopted Heraclitus's Logos and fire cosmology, developing them into a systematic physics and ethics
Neoplatonism systematized and mystified Plato's metaphysics — the Forms became stages in the emanation from the One
Scholasticism applied Aristotelian logic and metaphysics to theological questions — reason in the service of faith
German Idealism arose as attempts to complete and overcome Kant's critical philosophy — Fichte, Schelling, then Hegel
Husserl saw phenomenology as radicalizing Descartes — bracketing everything except the structures of consciousness itself
Logical Positivism is radical empiricism armed with modern logic — only verifiable statements are meaningful
Philosophy of science inherits empiricism's core question: how do we know the world through observation?
Analytic ethics began by refining utilitarianism — Moore, Sidgwick, and later Singer continued the consequentialist tradition
Existentialism specializes phenomenology toward the structures of human existence — anxiety, freedom, authenticity, death
Postmodernism radicalizes Nietzsche's perspectivism — if God is dead, so are all grand narratives and objective truths
Nagarjuna's Madhyamaka is the most rigorous philosophical elaboration of the Buddha's teaching on dependent origination
Vedanta is the culmination of Hindu philosophical inquiry — the 'end of the Vedas' and the school's deepest metaphysics
Effective altruism applies analytic ethics to practical questions of maximizing well-being with limited resources
Philosophy of AI extends philosophy of mind — can machines be conscious? What is the relationship between computation and thought?
Critical Theory is Marxism updated for late capitalism — cultural critique, ideology, and the administered society
Zen is a distinctly East Asian specialization of Buddhism emphasizing direct experience over textual study
Yoga philosophy systematizes the practices of meditation and concentration within Hindu metaphysics
Aquinas is the pinnacle of Scholasticism — the Summa Theologiae is its most complete and enduring achievement
Ockham represents late Scholasticism's nominalist turn — his razor cut away the metaphysical excesses of his predecessors
Socrates' trial and execution (399 BCE) traumatized Plato and drove him to write the Republic and found the Academy
Hume's skepticism about causation was the specific trigger that woke Kant from his 'dogmatic slumber' and produced the Critiques
Without Hegel's dialectic there is no Marx — historical materialism is the dialectic applied to economic conditions
Frege's invention of predicate logic (1879) made the analytic tradition possible — formal analysis of language became philosophy's core method
Nietzsche's proclamation that God is dead created the crisis of meaning that existentialism addresses
Pre-Socratic cosmology set the stage — Socrates shifted philosophy from nature to ethics, but inherited their rational method
Descartes's methodical doubt was a revolt against Scholastic authority — modern philosophy begins in the rejection of tradition
Kant's critical philosophy generated German Idealism — Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel all began as responses to the three Critiques
Husserl's phenomenological method directly produced Heidegger's existential ontology — Being and Time emerged from Husserl's seminars
The Tractatus directly inspired the Vienna Circle's verification principle — though Wittgenstein distanced himself from their interpretation
Hume revived ancient Pyrrhonian skepticism in modern form — suspension of judgment about causal necessity
Kant's ethics echoes Stoic themes — duty, reason, and the good will as the only unconditional good
Heidegger engaged with Taoism late in life — parallels between Gelassenheit (letting-be) and wu wei (non-action)
Nagarjuna's tetralemma and Pyrrho's suspension of judgment share a family resemblance — Pyrrho may have encountered Indian philosophy
Confucius and Aristotle share virtue ethics — moral cultivation through practice, the mean, and practical wisdom
Schopenhauer was the first major Western philosopher to engage seriously with Buddhist and Hindu thought — life as suffering, will as desire
Analytic philosophy of mind and phenomenology both address consciousness — from opposite methodological directions
Pragmatism inherits empiricism's emphasis on experience but rejects the spectator theory of knowledge — knowing is doing
Deep ecology echoes Taoist themes — nature has intrinsic value, harmony with the natural world, rejection of anthropocentrism
Buddhist interdependence (pratityasamutpada) resonates with ecological thinking — all beings arise together in mutual dependence
Averroes and Ibn Rushd are the same person — the medieval Latin and Arabic names for the philosopher from Cordoba
Avicenna belongs to both medieval and Islamic philosophical traditions — a bridge between East and West
Feminist philosophy engages critically with postmodernism — Butler's gender performativity draws on Foucault and Derrida
Later Wittgenstein and the pragmatists converge — meaning as use, philosophy as therapy, anti-foundationalism
Formal logic is the intellectual ancestor of AI — from Leibniz's dream of a universal calculus to modern machine reasoning
